invariant_definition: A divine name that becomes, in the Vaiṣṇava Vedāntic schools, a primary designation of the highest reality. The root sense of pervasion matters: Viṣṇu is the indwelling, world-sustaining, and world-governing Lord, not merely a mythic preserver opposed to a creator and destroyer. The term sits within a technical network that includes *Nārāyaṇa*, *Paramātman*, *Bhagavān*, *antaryāmin*, and *avatāra*. Viśiṣṭādvaita and Tattva-vāda (Dvaita) identify Viṣṇu-Nārāyaṇa straightforwardly with *brahman* and *Īśvara*; Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇavism places Viṣṇu within a fuller theology in which Kṛṣṇa is *svayaṃ-bhagavān* and Viṣṇu is a real divine expansion, not a lesser fiction. Advaita accepts Viṣṇu as a real form of *Īśvara* and a proper object of *upāsanā*, but not as the unique final description of *nirguṇa brahman*. The term therefore cannot be translated by the mythology-manual phrase "god of preservation" without erasing the metaphysical claims actually made around it.

per_school.definition: In Madhusūdana's Advaita, Viṣṇu can be named as the very *satya-jñāna-sukha* reality that shines when *mithyā-bandha* is shaken off; the personal name does not obstruct *akhaṇḍa-brahman* but serves as one of its theologically potent designators [[AS 1](cite://madhusudana/advaita-siddhi/1)]. Devotion to Mukunda is likewise treated as a genuine *parama-puruṣārtha* within the Advaita economy, so Viṣṇu is not reduced to a merely dispensable pedagogical fiction within *upāsanā*-language [[BR 1.1](cite://madhusudana/bhakti-rasayana/1.1)].

Rāmānuja's opening salutation already names Viṣṇu as the one qualified by the entirety of *cit* and *acit* and as the repository of *nirmalānantakalyāṇa-guṇa*s [[VS §1](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§1)]. The same treatise states that Vedāntic sentences aim to disclose the *antaryāmin*, explicitly glossed as the one Nārāyaṇa, together with the self's *upāsanā* of him and the blissful *brahmānubhava* that follows [[VS §4](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§4)]. Viṣṇu-Nārāyaṇa is therefore the *sarvātman*, *parama-brahman*, and *puruṣottama* whose relation to *cit* and *acit* is articulated through *antaryāmitva* and *śarīra-śarīrin* *sāmānādhikaraṇya* [[VS §6](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§6)].

Madhva makes Viṣṇu the single *svatantra-tattva*; all other reality falls on the *paratantra* side of the basic ontological divide [[TS 1](cite://madhva/tattva-sankhyana/1)]. This dependence is not merely cosmological but soteriological: Viṣṇu gives knowledge to the ignorant, liberation to knowers, and bliss to the liberated [[Anuvy. 1.1.13-15](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.1.13-15)]. In the *Viṣṇu-Tattva-Vinirṇaya*, Viṣṇu is *nirdoṣa*, *nityapūrṇākhilaguṇa*, and one whose *guṇa* and *kriyā* are his very *svarūpa* rather than adventitious predicates [[VTV 3.1](cite://madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.1)] [[VTV 3.12](cite://madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.12)]. Liberation is accordingly proximity to Viṣṇu as the all-*guṇa*-full lord, never absorption that annuls the hierarchy between the independent Lord and dependent selves [[VTV 3.24](cite://madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.24)] [[TS 11](cite://madhva/tattva-sankhyana/11)].

In Jīva's Gauḍīya theology, the supreme destination named by the Bhāgavata's *nārāyaṇa-pada* is glossed as the lotus feet of Śrī Kṛṣṇa in Vaikuṇṭha, so Viṣṇu-Nārāyaṇa is not a rival absolute to Kṛṣṇa but a real divine name gathered into Kṛṣṇa's status as the highest Bhagavān [[JS §273](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§273)]. Jīva can therefore gloss "Viṣṇu" itself as Śrī Kṛṣṇa manifest everywhere, while still treating Bhagavān alone as the object of *parama-prema* and final attainment [[JS BhP 10.35 gloss](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/bhp-10.35.16-17-gloss)] [[PS §45](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§45)].

Nimbārka does not leave the highest Brahman under an indeterminate title: the *Daśa-Ślokī* identifies the faultless, all-auspicious supreme Brahman directly as Kṛṣṇa-Hari, with the *vyūha*s as his forms and Rādhā abiding at his left side [[DaŚ 4](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/4)] [[DaŚ 5](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/5)]. The same dossier makes the *jīva* *hareḥ adhīna*, so Viṣṇu-Hari is not merely the recipient of worship but the independent Lord to whom the sentient plurality stands in *svābhāvika-bhedābheda* dependence [[DaŚ 1](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/1)] [[VPS 2.3.42](cite://nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/2.3.42)].

In Vallabha's *śuddhādvaita*, the functional place occupied elsewhere by Viṣṇu is borne by Brahman as Puruṣottama: Brahman is the *samavāyi-kāraṇa* of the world, the *jīva* is Brahman's own *aṃśa*, and liberation is attainment of Puruṣottama rather than absorption into an attribute-free absolute [[AB 1.1.4a](cite://vallabha/anubhasya/1.1.4a)] [[AB 2.3.43-45a](cite://vallabha/anubhasya/2.3.43-45a)] [[AB 1.1.12-19a](cite://vallabha/anubhasya/1.1.12-19a)]. The school's specific ontological move is *avikṛta-pariṇāma*: the Lord really manifests the world without undergoing intrinsic modification [[AB 1.4.26a](cite://vallabha/anubhasya/1.4.26a)].

Classical Sāṃkhya does not give Viṣṇu a doctrinal role; its nearest analogue is the rejected hypothesis of Īśvara [[SS 1.92](cite://kapila/samkhya-sutra/1.92)]. The *Sāṃkhya-Sūtra* says both that the fruition of results does not depend on an Īśvara-overseer because karma itself suffices, and that Īśvara is not established for lack of pramāṇa [[SS 5.2](cite://kapila/samkhya-sutra/5.2)] [[SS 5.10](cite://kapila/samkhya-sutra/5.10)].

Yoga's functional analogue to Viṣṇu is Īśvara: a *puruṣa-viśeṣa* untouched by *kleśa*, *karma*, *vipāka*, and *āśaya*, whose signifier is praṇava [[YS 1.24](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.24)] [[YS 1.27](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.27)]. Vyāsa then construes *īśvarapraṇidhāna* as a *bhakti-viśeṣa* by which the yogin is favored by Īśvara and rapidly gains samādhi, so the school admits a real Lord without technically identifying that Lord as Viṣṇu in the core sūtra-bhāṣya dossier [[YBh 1.23](cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.23)] [[YBh 1.24](cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.24)].

Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika likewise works with Īśvara-Bhagavān rather than a sectarian Viṣṇu-term: Udayana says that Bhagavān is known by scripture and inference, directly seen by some, and sought by sages through sacrifice, tapas, yama, niyama, and yoga [[NK 5.685-689](cite://udayana/nyaya-kusumanjali/5.685-689)]. The school's concern is thus the prameya-status of a rationally defensible Lord, not a separate technical ontology built around the divine name Viṣṇu [[NK 5.685-689](cite://udayana/nyaya-kusumanjali/5.685-689)].

Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā does not treat Viṣṇu as a supreme metaphysical lord; it subordinates deity-language to ritual injunction [[MS 1.1.2](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.1.2)] [[MS 9.1.9](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/9.1.9)]. Jaimini defines *dharma* as what is characterized by *codanā* and grounds it in *śabda*, while also stating that the sacrifice-act is primary and that the *devatā*-text has *guṇa*-status relative to the rite [[MS 1.1.2](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.1.2)] [[MS 1.3.1](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.3.1)] [[MS 9.1.9](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/9.1.9)]. The relevant analogue to a Viṣṇu-doctrine is therefore negative: even where a deity is named, its role is functionally tied to sacrificial performance rather than installed as the independent object of final metaphysical inquiry [[MS 6.3.18-19](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/6.3.18-19)] [[MS 9.1.9](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/9.1.9)].

per_school.not_applicable_reason: The listed Mādhyamaka texts treat emptiness, dependent arising, and dialectic; Viṣṇu or a positive theistic analogue is not a load-bearing category in this dossier.
The listed Yogācāra and Buddhist epistemological texts analyze vijñapti, cognition, and pramāṇa rather than Viṣṇu or any school-defining theistic analogue.

per_school.definition: Trika assigns Viṣṇu a definite but subordinate place within the graded manifestation of consciousness [[ĪPV 3.1.2](cite://abhinavagupta/ipv/3.1.2-comment)] [[ĪPV 3.2.1](cite://abhinavagupta/ipv/3.2.1-comment)]. Abhinavagupta warns that the upāsya deities called Sadāśiva and Īśvara are not to be confused with Brahmā or Viṣṇu merely on the strength of name-similarity, since those latter belong to a distinct lower order of manifestation [[ĪPV 3.1.2](cite://abhinavagupta/ipv/3.1.2-comment)]. He then states more exactly that Rudra presides over the recollective condition of pramātṛtā, whereas in the outward spread of differentiated objects the *adhiṣṭhātṛ-devatā* is Viṣṇu; Viṣṇu therefore names a real phase of manifestation, not the absolute itself [[ĪPV 3.2.1](cite://abhinavagupta/ipv/3.2.1-comment)].

translator_note: Avoid "the god of preservation." That gloss imports the Trimūrti classroom schema and obscures the fact that Vaiṣṇava Vedānta uses Viṣṇu as a name of the highest reality itself. Even where another school ranks Viṣṇu differently, Viṣṇu is still a real divine being, not a symbolic personification.
